content:
14 min read
Just now

--

It starts with a feeling you can’t quite spell. Embeddings, distance, the great bargain, and the one honest number — explained from zero, with one late-night movie search we chase all the way down.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Quiver, Explained, a 5-part series. You’re reading Part 1.

• What Is a Vector Database? ← you are here

• Inside the Engine: SIMD, graphs, and the 32× memory trick

• Surviving kill -9: durability & security

• The Numbers, Honestly: benchmarking at a fixed recall

• Beyond Search: RAG, ops & scaling out

It’s late. You’ve got the couch, the good blanket, and exactly one movie left in you before you fall asleep with the remote on your chest. And you know the kind of movie you want tonight. Not a title. A feeling. One of those ones where two people clearly can’t stand each other, and then somehow can, and you spend ninety minutes grinning at your own television like a fool.
So you type the feeling into the search box. “Funny movie where two people fall in love by accident.”
And the box just looks at you. Like a dog that heard a word it knows but can’t work out which one.
Here’s what went wrong, and it’s the whole reason I’m writing this. That search box wasn’t reading your meaning. It was reading your letters. It went hunting for the exact words “funny” and “love” and “accident” stamped somewhere in a description, and the movie that would’ve been perfect, How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days, describes itself with completely different ones. A magazine columnist. A bet. Ten days. Not a single word in common with what you typed. Same feeling exactly, and completely invisible to the search.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days (2003), Paramount PicturesThat little gap, between what you meant and what you could spell, turns out to be one of the most valuable problems in software. Closing it is how Netflix knows what you’ll want next, how a chatbot finds the one useful paragraph in a 400-page manual, how your photos app finds every picture of your dog when you’ve never once typed the word “dog.” And a few years ago it became my problem too, because I went and built one of the machines that closes it.
It’s called Quiver. It’s free, it’s open-source, and over these five articles I’m going to take it completely apart and show you how it works. Here’s my one promise: I’ll assume you know nothing about any of this. No databases, no math, no AI. If you can follow a movie recommendation, you can follow this.
We need four ideas to get there. How you turn a feeling into numbers. How you measure when two things are close. The clever bargain that makes all of it fast. And the single number that stops everyone in this field from lying to you, and to themselves. I’ll hand them over one at a time, and I’ll hand you one thread to hold onto the whole way down: that late-night wish, made real. A search that finds movies that feel like How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days. No shared words. Just the feeling.
Let’s start with a map.
Why search has always been a little bit dumb
The search you’ve used your whole life matches spelling. You ask it for “wedding,” it finds the things with the letters w-e-d-d-i-n-g sitting inside them. That's genuinely perfect for some jobs. Looking up an order number, finding an email address, searching your own texts for the word "pizza," you want it to be a stickler about the exact letters. You'd be annoyed if it got creative.
It falls apart the moment you ask for something by how it feels. Two descriptions can share zero words and mean nearly the same thing. Two can share almost every word and mean the opposite. “Not scary at all” and “scary” differ by one little word and point in completely opposite directions, and a letter-matcher can’t tell. Letters are just the wrong tool for the job. We need to search the idea.
That’s the whole quest, and I’ll keep saying it like a chorus, so here it is for the first time: search the meaning, not the spelling.
The big idea: turning meaning into numbers
This next bit is the one genuinely mind-bending idea underneath all of modern AI search. Everything else is just plumbing. So read it twice.
You can take almost anything (a sentence, a photo, a song, a movie) and turn it into a list of numbers that captures what it means.
That list of numbers has an intimidating name, embedding, which we’ll mostly ignore. Picture it instead as the movie’s fingerprint: a long row of numbers that somehow holds its whole vibe.
How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days → [0.12, 0.88, -0.07, 0.41, … ] (about 768 numbers)
What are the numbers? Loosely, each one is a score on some invisible question. How romantic is this? How funny? How dark? How hopeful? Except a real system invents thousands of these little questions on its own, far weirder and more specific than anything you or I would think to ask, and it does not tell us what they are. (Yes, 768 of them. No, nobody can picture 768 dimensions, including the people who do this for a living. They just nod and carry on, and so will we.)
Here is the only part you have to remember, and it’s the part that feels like magic:
Things that mean similar things end up with similar numbers.
So How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days and The Proposal land right next to each other. How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days and Tombstone land a continent apart. Meaning stops being a vague, fuzzy thing you can only gesture at. It becomes a place. A spot on a map.
And that’s the picture to carry the rest of the way:
A gigantic map with every movie in the world pinned to it. The ones that feel alike drift into the same neighborhoods. There’s a cozy rom-com district over here, a brooding sci-fi quarter across town, a dusty western out past the edge.
Suddenly “movies like How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days” isn’t a wishy-washy request anymore. It’s a physical thing you can do. Drop a pin where your movie sits, and look at whatever’s parked closest to it. A normal map has two directions, north and east. This one has hundreds. That sounds exotic and it really isn’t. It just means there are more directions in which two movies can be neighbors.
Now, my favorite example in this whole series, because it proves the map is reading the soul of a movie and not the label on the box:
Matthew McConaughey is the lead in both How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days and Interstellar. Same guy. Same face on the poster. And yet on this map those two movies sit nowhere near each other, one deep in rom-com country and the other three galaxies away, because the map couldn’t care less who’s in the cast. Meanwhile Interstellar and Inception, no shared actors, different directors, land practically on top of each other, because they feel like cousins. The map reads what a movie is, not who showed up to film it.
Where do these magic numbers come from? From a separate piece of AI called an embedding model. Think of it as a very well-read film critic who has seen everything and answers instantly. You hand it a movie’s description, it hands you back the fingerprint. And this is exactly where Quiver’s job begins: the moment you have the fingerprints.
I want to be clear about that boundary, because it’s a real design choice and not me being lazy. Quiver doesn’t come with the critic. You bring your own. That’s on purpose. These critics get replaced by better ones every few months, and if I’d welded my database to one specific critic, the database would’ve gone stale the week a better one came out. So Quiver stays out of that business entirely and does the part that’s genuinely hard and genuinely lasting: keeping millions of those fingerprints safe and tiny, and finding the nearest ones absurdly fast. The critic is a part you can swap. The database is the thing you build your house on.
“Close,” but how close, exactly?
If meaning is a place, then “similar” just means “nearby.” Which sounds simple until you have to actually measure it, and it turns out there’s more than one honest way to measure the distance between two points, and they don’t always agree.
There are three. You really only need to feel the first two; the third is for completeness.

• Cosine asks, “are these two pointing the same direction?” It ignores how far. This is the usual pick for text and meaning.

• Euclidean (also called L2) asks, “how far apart are they, by a straight ruler?” Good for images, maps, physical things.

• Dot product folds direction and size together at once. Handy for recommendations.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two honest ways to measure “close” — the angle between two arrows (cosine) versus the straight-line gap between two points (Euclidean / L2).The picture does more than I can. Cosine looks at the direction two things point and shrugs off the length. The ruler just measures the gap.
For text, cosine usually wins, and there’s a homely reason why. Imagine a sprawling 2,000-word review of Interstellar sitting next to a one-line tweet about it. Same opinion, wildly different lengths. As arrows on the map they point the same way, but one is much longer than the other. Cosine throws the length away and asks the only question you actually cared about: are we pointing the same direction? The tweet and the essay come out as neighbors, which is exactly right.
There’s a small, satisfying trick buried in here that I can’t resist mentioning. If you shrink every fingerprint down to the same length first, cosine quietly turns into the dot product. Same answer, simpler sum. So the engine doesn’t need three separate tools for three measures. It needs two, and the third rides along for free. I have a weakness for designs where the hard part turns out to be small, and you’ll see that weakness all over Quiver.
The wall everybody hits
Here’s where this stops being a cute idea and becomes an actual engineering problem, which is the part I find fun.
Finding the nearest movie sounds trivial. Hold your movie up next to every other movie, measure each gap, keep the closest. Done. With a thousand movies it’s instant and you’d never think about it again.
Then your thousand movies becomes a hundred million documents. Now that same “just check everything” approach has to do something like 76 billion little multiplications. For one search. While a real person waits. That’s not slow, that’s never. You’d be retired before it finished.
So every system in this entire field, mine included, makes the same quiet deal with the devil:
Approximate search: stop insisting on the perfect closest ten. In exchange, go 100 to 1,000 times faster while still being right about 95 to 99 percent of the time.
That trade, a tiny and controllable sacrifice in accuracy for a colossal gain in speed, is the beating heart of the whole business. You don’t get garbage back. You get almost the perfect answer, fast, with a dial you can turn to decide exactly how “almost.” Everything clever in the rest of this series happens inside that one bargain.
The one number that keeps everyone honest
The accuracy you keep has a name, and it’s the most important word in these five articles, so I’m putting it in a box:
Recall. Of the ten movies that are truly the closest, how many did the fast search actually bring back?
Recall of 0.96 means it found, on average, 9.6 of the real top ten. Checking everything always scores a perfect 10 out of 10, and pays for it in time nobody has. The fast way trades a sliver of that perfection for a mountain of speed.
Why do I care so much about this one number? Because speed without it is a magic trick. I could build you the fastest movie search on Earth this afternoon: it returns ten random movies, instantly. Blistering speed. Completely useless. So any speed claim that doesn’t come stapled to a recall number is, frankly, a lie with good lighting. “A thousand searches a second” means nothing until somebody finishes the sentence: “…while still finding 95% of the right answers.”
That’s a hou
